summary: In response to Chris Dixon's concern that proprietary services are winning over open protocols, and Ev Williams' pessimistic comparison to radio and TV's centralization, Asparouhova argues the internet may be different. She identifies four forces pushing toward openness: empowered content creators who can leave platforms, low barriers to building products, the rise of patronage models, and blockchain technology. Her key insight is that unlike traditional media, the internet isn't a single distribution channel but an amalgamation of many, with new ones constantly emerging. This means the innovation-to-centralization cycle repeats rapidly, with empires built and broken overnight.
key_points: Content creators with sufficient audience can always take their followers elsewhere, creating tension with centralized platforms
Patronage models (Patreon, direct fan support) could free creators from platform dependence
The internet fundamentally differs from radio/TV because it's not one distribution channel but many channels constantly being created
Both centralization pessimists and decentralization optimists may be right—the cycle just repeats faster on the internet
money_quotes: In the end, with enough critical mass, mini moguls can always take their audience elsewhere.
The easier it is for anybody to shape the internet, the more agency people will want over their ideas.
Radio or television ARE distribution channels; the internet is an amalgamation of many of those channels - many radios or televisions - and new ones are being created all the time.
Empires are built and broken overnight, and the pendulum will continue to swing back and forth.
If successful, patronage could wean more creators off of centralized platforms.
key_insight: The internet differs from traditional media because it contains multitudes of distribution channels constantly being created, so while each channel may centralize, the rapid cycle of innovation means the pendulum keeps swinging.
